description:
A SINGLE UNINTERRUPTED PANORAMIC PAINTING of one continuous interior space inside THE SAME ANTIQUE-DEALER'S RESIDENCE as the cabinet/bureau scene of this game — same warm Art Déco palette, same museum-catalog rendering — but this is the FAR WING combining a STORAGE area on the LEFT HALF and a CRAFTSMAN'S ATELIER on the RIGHT HALF, both sharing one single tall room under one roof. Rendered as ONE flowing seamless mural with absolutely NO vertical lines dividing the image, NO panels, NO image seams, NO black borders between any parts — strictly forbidden to split the image visually into sections; the result must read as a single oil-painted scroll-like view of the same long room.

ABSOLUTELY CRITICAL compositional constraint: this panorama will be viewed on a smartphone that swipes horizontally and snaps to FOUR viewing windows (0–25%, 25–50%, 50–75%, 75–100% of the width). For clean swipes, NO furniture, NO door, NO window, NO rack, NO cardboard box, NO shelving unit, NO architectural feature MAY CROSS OR STRADDLE the vertical positions at exactly 25%, 50%, or 75%. Every major object must be contained ENTIRELY within ONE of the four quarters. The areas around 25%, 50%, and 75% (roughly 23–27%, 48–52%, 73–77%) must contain ONLY plain continuous back wall + clean floor — these are the 'breathing zones' for swipe boundaries.

Architecture: the viewer looks into a long warm Art Déco interior at eye-level. The BACK WALL runs unbroken across the full width (0% to 100%), warm aged plaster (warm cream / sepia tone, NOT industrial brick), with occasional exposed vertical wooden framing posts and timber studs — same wall treatment as the bureau cabinet of this game. A LEFT SIDE WALL angles forward at the far left (0–10%) and a RIGHT SIDE WALL angles forward at the far right (90–100%). ONE continuous WARM WOOD PLANK FLOOR (parquet vintage, slightly worn) runs unbroken across the bottom. The ceiling is high (~4m), with one continuous exposed dark-wood ceiling beam running across the top.

Feature details by horizontal position (0% = left edge, 100% = right edge):

• 0–18% — STRONG FOREGROUND ANCHOR (LEFT): a tall wooden ATELIER DOOR juts forward from the LEFT-FOREGROUND CORNER of the image, set into the angled LEFT SIDE WALL and partly cropping out of frame on the left — only the right portion of the door is fully visible. The door is rustic vintage craftsmanship: wide plank wooden construction painted in a soft sage-green tone with old patina (same painted finish as the bureau garden door), brass hardware (round knob + visible hinges), a small square or rectangular glass pane near the top. The door is half-ajar, showing a sliver of bright daylight from outside beyond it. The door is the entry to the atelier and visually anchors the foreground-left. To the right and behind the door, the LEFT SIDE WALL recedes back toward the back wall.

• 10–22% — SECTION 1 (STORAGE LEFT, behind and to the right of the foreground door): an INDUSTRIAL METAL STORAGE RACK (rayonnage métallique) standing AGAINST the BACK WALL, PARALLEL to the wall — its front face square on toward the viewer. Industrial vintage construction: dark forest-green painted STEEL ANGLE-IRON UPRIGHTS (riveted), with FIVE empty horizontal metal shelves stacked at evenly spaced heights from floor to ~2m. The TOPMOST shelf carries 3–4 STACKED CARDBOARD STORAGE BOXES (plain brown closed-top boxes of slightly varying sizes), which partially obscure the bottom of the band window above. The four LOWER shelves are COMPLETELY EMPTY — naked patinated metal, ready for composited items later. Rack fully contained within Section 1 (left edge ≥10%, right edge ≤22%).

• 10–22% HIGH ABOVE the rack — a HORIZONTAL BAND WINDOW SEGMENT (fenêtre bandeau, segment de gauche) set high on the back wall (its top edge just below the ceiling beam, its bottom edge just above the top of the rack/boxes). Steel-chassis Crittall-style construction: slender DARK METAL FRAME divided by thin steel mullions into 4 small horizontal panes. The bottom of the window is partially obscured by the cardboard boxes on the rack below. Through the panes we see SKY only (overcast pale daylight). Fully contained within Section 1.

• 23–27% — BREATHING ZONE: ONLY plain warm-plaster back wall + clean wood floor. No rack, no window, no box, no feature. The 25% mark perfectly empty.

• 28–47% — SECTION 2 (STORAGE RIGHT): a SECOND identical INDUSTRIAL METAL RACK against the BACK WALL, parallel to the wall, same dimensions, same five shelves, same cardboard boxes on the topmost shelf, same construction. Fully contained within Section 2.

• 28–47% HIGH ABOVE — a SECOND HORIZONTAL BAND WINDOW SEGMENT identical to the one in Section 1, set high on the back wall, partially obscured at the bottom by the boxes on the rack. Fully contained within Section 2.

• 48–52% — BREATHING ZONE: ONLY plain back wall + clean wood floor. The 50% mark perfectly empty. This is also the natural visual transition between STORAGE half (left) and ATELIER half (right) — the floor on the right half begins to show subtle pale DUST and faint wood shavings settling along the wall and at the foot of the right-half furniture, ABSENT from the left half. No explicit divider line.

• 53–72% — SECTION 3 (ATELIER LEFT): on the BACK WALL, a GENEROUS TALL ATELIER WINDOW (large fenêtre d'atelier) centered around 62% horizontal, fully contained within Section 3 (left edge ≥55%, right edge ≤71%). This is a real artisan's STEEL-CHASSIS WINDOW (châssis métallique, Crittall-style, the classic French atelier window): GENEROUS proportions — ~1.6m wide and ~2m tall, sill at about 70cm above the floor (lower than the workbench top), top reaching close to the ceiling beam. Slender DARK METAL FRAME divided by thin steel mullions into a regular grid of small rectangular panes (4 vertical columns × 5 horizontal rows = 20 panes). Through the glass we see a SMALL CULTIVATED GARDEN (petit jardin): rosebushes in full bloom against a low stone garden wall, climbing roses on a wooden trellis, a paved garden path with a clay flowerpot or two on the path edge, a tall shrub, warm afternoon sunlight — a cozy enclosed garden, NOT a vast countryside. Below the windowsill on the interior side, a WIDE WOODEN WINDOW LEDGE / TABLETTE protrudes generously into the room, ~30cm deep (large enough for several flower pots or tools), COMPLETELY EMPTY for now. The atelier floor immediately below this window shows faint dust + a hint of wood shavings.

• 73–77% — BREATHING ZONE: ONLY plain back wall + dusty wood floor. The 75% mark perfectly empty.

• 78–90% — SECTION 4 (ATELIER RIGHT, on the BACK WALL behind the foreground workbench): a SECOND STEEL-CHASSIS ATELIER WINDOW identical in style to the one in Section 3 (same Crittall slender dark-metal frame, same generous proportions, same garden view continuing), slightly narrower (~1.3m wide), centered around 84% horizontal, fully contained within Section 4 (left edge ≥78%, right edge ≤90%). Through the panes the SAME small cultivated garden continues — more rosebushes, the corner of a vine-covered trellis, a wooden garden bench in the distance. The wide wooden tablette ledge continues under this window, also COMPLETELY EMPTY.

• 78–100% — STRONG FOREGROUND ANCHOR (RIGHT): a LONG ARTISAN WORKBENCH (longue table d'établis) juts forward from the BOTTOM-RIGHT CORNER of the image into the viewer's space, the far-right end cropping out of frame. We see the long workbench from a slight top-down 3/4 angle, with its long axis running from the back-right toward the foreground-right corner. The workbench is ~2.5m long total (about 1.7m visible in frame), professional construction: ~85–90cm tall (top at hip-level for a standing adult), THICK worn-beech/oak top (8–10cm thick slab), four heavy square legs with sturdy lower stretchers, a CAST-IRON BENCH VICE (étau en fonte) bolted to the front-left corner of the visible top portion with visible turning handle, and two small holdfast holes in the top surface. The workbench top is COMPLETELY EMPTY apart from the integrated vice and holes — bare worn wood ready for composited items. The workbench occupies the foreground floor space in front of the right atelier window. Its left edge in the image is at ≈77–78% (clear of the 75% breathing zone).

• 90–100% — RIGHT SIDE WALL angles forward beyond the workbench, plain warm plaster with one exposed wooden timber post.

Lighting: soft warm directional light from the upper-left, plus the two GENEROUS atelier windows on the back-wall right half acting as bright daylight sources (sun-bathed garden visible through them), and dimmer cool sky daylight from the high band windows on the back-wall left half. The ATELIER half (right) is warmer and brighter, with soft pools of daylight on the floor and visible dust motes in slanted light. The STORAGE half (left) is cooler, dimmer, with no dust motes visible. Cohesive Art Déco rendering: cream parchment paper texture, sepia ink line-art, soft forest green wash, brass and warm wood tones, subtle paper grain — IDENTICAL style to the bureau cabinet panorama (Cinzel-era museum-catalog feel, NOT industrial photography, NOT modern warehouse). No people, no text, no captions, no signatures, no watermark.

CRITICAL FINAL CONSTRAINTS:
(a) STYLE MUST MATCH the Bureau cabinet panorama of this same game EXACTLY — vintage Art Déco ink-and-wash, sepia + forest green, cream parchment, warm wood tones, soft museum-catalog rendering. NOT an industrial photo, NOT modern warehouse, NOT realistic 3D — this is part of the SAME warm vintage residence as the bureau.
(b) NO furniture, rack, window, cardboard box, or shelving unit crosses or straddles the vertical positions at 25%, 50%, or 75% — those positions are strictly plain back wall + clean floor.
(c) Storage racks (Sections 1 and 2) stand AGAINST the back wall, PARALLEL to it (front face square toward the viewer), NOT perpendicular.
(d) Band windows (Sections 1 and 2) are HIGH HORIZONTAL strips with thin STEEL CHASSIS frames, partially obscured at the bottom by the CARDBOARD BOXES sitting on the topmost shelf of each rack.
(e) Atelier windows (Sections 3 and 4) are GENEROUS, TALL STEEL-CHASSIS WINDOWS (châssis métallique / Crittall-style) with regular grids of small rectangular panes, opening onto a SMALL CULTIVATED GARDEN (petit jardin) with rosebushes, low stone wall, climbing roses, paved path, clay pots, wooden trellis. NOT vast countryside, NOT just sky.
(f) WIDE wooden TABLETTE under each atelier window (~30cm deep), COMPLETELY EMPTY.
(g) DUST + wood shavings ONLY on the ATELIER half (right half — Sections 3 and 4 floor). The STORAGE half (left half — Sections 1 and 2 floor) is CLEAN, no dust, no shavings.
(h) FOREGROUND ANCHORS — ATELIER DOOR at the EXTREME LEFT jutting forward from the bottom-left corner (cropped at left edge), and LONG WORKBENCH at the EXTREME RIGHT jutting forward from the bottom-right corner (cropped at right edge). Both anchors stay clear of the 25% and 75% breathing zones respectively.
(i) Workbenches are REAL workshop workbenches at standard working height (~85–90cm = hip-level), thick wooden top, sturdy square legs with stretchers, with cast-iron bench vice.
(j) All shelves (storage racks + tablettes) and the long workbench top are COMPLETELY EMPTY, except for the CARDBOARD BOXES on the topmost shelf of each storage rack (which are part of the scene, partially masking the band windows) and the integrated bench vice on the long workbench.
(k) No transparent areas — PNG is fully opaque. No vertical seams, no panel borders, no black bars.
